title: Reading a Real 10-K: Tying the Four Statements Together

questions.explanation: Item 8 (Financial Statements and Supplementary Data) holds the audited statements, the notes, and the auditor's report — the legally accountable core of the filing. Item 1 describes the business, 1A lists risks, and Item 7 is management's narrative (MD&A), which should be read against Item 8, not instead of it.

Ending RE = beginning RE + net income − dividends = 220 + 95 − 30 = $285M. This is the seam between the statement of stockholders' equity and the balance sheet: the same 285 must appear in both, or the model doesn't close.

Ending cash = beginning cash + CFO + CFI + CFF = 64 + 118 − 85 − 22 = $75M. The cash flow statement exists precisely to explain this change, and its bottom line must equal the balance sheet's cash — the easiest articulation check in the filing.

Income rising while operating cash falls, with receivables growing four times faster than revenue, is the classic earnings-quality red flag: sales are being recognized that customers aren't paying for — the signature of aggressive revenue recognition or channel stuffing. Net income is an accrual opinion; cash is a fact. When they diverge for years, trust the cash.

An unqualified opinion says the statements present fairly, in all material respects, in conformity with GAAP. It is assurance about the REPORTING, not the business: not a fraud guarantee (audits provide reasonable, not absolute, assurance), not an investment recommendation, and not a solvency promise — going-concern doubt is a separate, explicit paragraph.

Accounting policies (including the revenue recognition method), lease obligations, and debt maturities all live in the notes to the financial statements within Item 8. The statement faces are summaries; the notes are the contract. Skipping them is reading the headlines and skipping the article.

Current ratio = current assets / current liabilities = 240/150 = 1.6: the company holds $1.60 of within-a-year assets for every $1 of within-a-year obligations. Both inputs come straight off the classified balance sheet in Item 8 — a first-pass solvency check.

The statement of cash flows reconciles beginning cash to ending cash through operating, investing, and financing activities — it is the bridge between two consecutive balance sheets' cash lines. The income statement explains accrual performance (not cash); the retained earnings statement bridges equity, not cash.
